1.synthesis:
Hoshea son of Elah begins to reign in Samaria in the twelfth year of Ahaz king of Judah. The evaluation is negative but with a qualification unusual in the northern regnal notices: he does evil, but not as the kings of Israel before him. Against him comes up Shalmaneser king of Assyria, and Hoshea becomes his servant and pays tribute. But Hoshea conspires — he sends messengers to So king of Egypt and withholds tribute from Assyria. Shalmaneser imprisons Hoshea and besieges Samaria for three years. In the ninth year of Hoshea — the year is 722 BC — Samaria falls. The king of Assyria (now Sargon II in the Assyrian records, though the text simply says "the king of Assyria") carries Israel away to Assyria and places them in Halah, on the Habor, the river of Gozan, and in the cities of the Medes. The northern kingdom is gone.

John Gill notes the moderated condemnation of Hoshea — "not as the kings of Israel before him" — and some commentators speculate he may have removed the calf-shrines at Bethel and Dan that had defined northern kingship since Jeroboam I. Whatever his relative moderation, it was not sufficient to prevent the judgment long prophesied; the trajectory of the northern kingdom was by this point irreversible. Matthew Henry observes that Hoshea's conspiracy with Egypt — the classic move of a small power trying to play great powers against each other — is exactly the kind of faithless political calculation that Isaiah warned Judah against under Ahaz. Egypt is a "broken reed" that will pierce the hand of anyone who leans on it (Isa 36:6).

Keil and Delitzsch note the precision of the three-year siege: Samaria was a well-fortified city on a hill, and its resistance was significant. The deportation to Halah, Habor, and the cities of the Medes is historically documented — Sargon II's own inscriptions record the deportation of 27,290 Israelites from Samaria, though the actual number deported over the period may have been higher.
1.historical_context: The fall of Samaria in 722/721 BC is one of the most consequential events in Israelite history. Shalmaneser V began the siege; his successor Sargon II completed it and took credit in his own inscriptions. The Assyrian practice of deportation — established by Tiglath-Pileser III — involved removing the socially and economically significant segments of a conquered population (artisans, officials, military leaders) to other parts of the empire while importing new populations. The "ten lost tribes" of the northern kingdom's deportation became absorbed into the Mesopotamian and Median populations, their distinct Israelite identity dissolving over generations. The three-year siege (17:5) dates to 725–722 BC. Egypt's "So" (17:4) may be an Egyptian pharaoh or a city; the identification is uncertain.
1.literary_structure: This brief six-verse section narrates the end of the northern kingdom with deliberate economy: the conspiracy, the siege, and the deportation in compressed sequence. The narrator then pauses — the next section (vv. 7–23) provides the lengthy theological explanation for why this happened, the most extended theological commentary in the entire book of Kings. The bare narration of the fall precedes and demands the explanation; together they form the most important passage in Kings for understanding the Deuteronomistic theology of history.
1.theological_application: Calvin observes that the fall of Samaria was not a historical accident but the culmination of two centuries of covenant abandonment. The Deuteronomistic historian places the theological explanation at precisely this moment — not distributed through the centuries of failure but concentrated at the moment of final judgment — to ensure the reader understands what has happened. The church receives from this the sobering principle that institutional forms (a nation, a religious establishment, a church) can persist in external existence long after the covenant life has departed; their eventual end is not unjust but the outworking of what was already spiritually true. Matthew Henry urges that the fall of Samaria should function as a warning to those who have greater light and greater covenant obligations.
1.christology: The deportation of Israel to Assyria places God's covenant people in exile — scattered among the nations, the promised land lost, the temple of the northern kingdom destroyed. This prefigures the greater exile of the human condition: separated from God's presence, scattered by sin, unable by their own power to return. Christ's mission is described in the Servant Songs as gathering the scattered people: "It is too small a thing for you to be my servant to restore the tribes of Jacob and bring back those of Israel I have kept. I will also make you a light for the Gentiles, that my salvation may reach to the ends of the earth" (Isa 49:6). The scattered northern tribes, absorbed into the nations, are part of the "all nations" that the Son of Man comes to gather and restore.

7.synthesis:
The narrator pauses the historical narrative to provide the longest and most theologically explicit commentary in the entire book of Kings. Israel was exiled because they sinned against the Lord their God who brought them up out of Egypt — the Exodus is the covenant foundation whose violation is being adjudicated. The indictment is comprehensive: they feared other gods, walked in the customs of the nations the Lord had driven out and in those the kings of Israel had introduced, they built high places in every city, set up sacred stones and Asherah poles, burned incense to all the host of heaven, and served Baal. They practiced divination and sorcery, and sold themselves to do evil in the sight of the Lord, provoking him to anger. The Lord warned them through every prophet and seer: "Turn from your evil ways. Observe my commands and decrees." But they would not listen; they were stiff-necked like their ancestors. They rejected his statutes and the covenant he had made with their ancestors and the warnings he had given them; they followed worthless idols and became worthless themselves.

The text moves from the general indictment to specific catalog: they forsook all the commands of the Lord and made cast idols, two calves, an Asherah pole, worshiped all the starry hosts and served Baal. They sacrificed their sons and daughters in fire and practiced divination and sorcery — selling themselves to do evil and provoking the Lord to anger. Therefore the Lord was very angry with Israel and removed them from his presence. Only the tribe of Judah was left, but even Judah did not keep the commands of the Lord and followed the practices that Israel had introduced. So the Lord rejected all the people of Israel; he afflicted them and gave them into the hands of plunderers until he banished them from his presence.

John Gill traces the structure of this passage as a compressed covenant lawsuit: the Lord had fulfilled his obligations (the Exodus, the giving of the law, the land, the sending of prophets), while Israel had persistently failed theirs. Matthew Henry finds the phrase "they rejected his decrees and the covenant he had made with their ancestors and the warnings he had given them" to be the comprehensive indictment: word, covenant, and prophetic warning — all three avenues of divine communication were rejected. Keil and Delitzsch emphasize the phrase "they became worthless" after following worthless idols: the text is making a moral epistemological point, that the character of what is worshiped shapes the character of the worshiper.
7.historical_context: This theological explanation (17:7–23) is widely recognized as one of the most important passages for understanding the Deuteronomistic History's theological framework. It draws on the covenant theology of Deuteronomy — particularly the blessings and curses of Deuteronomy 28 — and the pattern of prophetic warning seen throughout Kings. The specific sins listed — high places, sacred stones, Asherah poles, worship of the host of heaven, Baal worship, child sacrifice, divination — constitute the comprehensive inventory of Canaanite religious practice that Israel adopted despite the Sinai prohibition. The reference to "what the Lord had commanded them not to do" (17:12) echoes the specific prohibitions of Leviticus and Deuteronomy. The note that Judah also did not keep the commands (17:19) anticipates the eventual Babylonian exile as the same theological logic applied to the south.
7.literary_structure: The passage is structured as a covenant lawsuit in three movements: historical recollection of God's saving acts (the Exodus), indictment of Israel's persistent violation, and the verdict (exile). The repetitive structure — "they did… and they did… and they did" — builds an overwhelming sense of accumulated wickedness. The conclusion — "the Lord removed them from his presence" — answers the problem of Israel's original calling to be a holy people before the Lord; those who refuse holiness cannot remain in the presence of the Holy One. The section ends with the dynasty of Jeroboam as the proximate cause: "they followed the sins of Jeroboam in all his sins that he committed; they did not depart from them."
7.theological_application: Calvin reads this passage as the definitive statement of the Deuteronomistic theology of history: nations that enjoy covenant privilege and reject covenant obligation face covenant curse. The warnings were not few or ambiguous — "every prophet and seer" warned Israel continuously. The stiff-necked refusal to hear is not ignorance but hardened will. Matthew Henry's application to the contemporary church is pointed: the same divine patience that sent prophet after prophet to Israel now sends Scripture, preaching, conscience, and providence as instruments of warning. Persistent rejection of these warnings, when they are clearly and repeatedly given, produces the same kind of judicial abandonment — not because God is cruel but because he is consistent. The phrase "they became worthless like the worthless idols they followed" is a profound anthropological observation: what we worship shapes what we become.
7.christology: The theological explanation for Israel's exile culminates in the note that the Lord "removed them from his presence" (17:18). The exile is the spatial enactment of the spiritual reality of covenant rupture — being removed from the land of God's presence to the land of the nations. Christ enters this condition of exile and removal: "He was despised and rejected by mankind" (Isa 53:3); he who knew no sin bore the exile-condition of sin, the abandonment expressed in "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matt 27:46). The restoration from exile — the return from being removed from God's presence — is accomplished in Christ who, having borne the removal, is restored to the right hand of the Father and draws his people into that same presence: "Where I am, my servant also will be" (John 12:26).

24.synthesis:
The king of Assyria brings people from Babylon, Cuthah, Avva, Hamath, and Sepharvaim and settles them in the cities of Samaria in place of the Israelites. They take possession of Samaria and live in its towns. At first they do not fear the Lord, and the Lord sends lions among them that kill some of them. A report reaches the king of Assyria: the nations you have settled in the towns of Samaria do not know what the god of the land requires, so he has sent lions among them. The king commands that one of the deported Israelite priests be sent back to teach them what the god of the land requires. A priest returns and lives at Bethel and teaches how to fear the Lord. But each national group makes its own gods and sets them up in the high places the Samaritans had made — each nation in the towns where they live. The narrator catalogs their deities: the Babylonians made Succoth Benoth, the Cuthites made Nergal, the Hamath men made Ashima, the Avvites made Nibhaz and Tartak, and the Sepharvites burned their children in fire to Adrammelech and Anammelech. They fear the Lord but also serve their own gods, "after the custom of the nations from among whom they had been carried away."

John Gill observes that this syncretistic settlement — the Samaritans of later tradition — becomes the ongoing religious problem that runs through the rest of Kings, Ezra, Nehemiah, and into the New Testament. The returned priest teaches from Bethel — ironically the site of Jeroboam's calf-shrines — producing a religion of mixed reverence for Yahweh and continued idol-worship. Matthew Henry notes the theological precision of the Lord's use of lions: the new settlers needed to learn "what the god of the land requires," and the lions function as a providential lesson that the land belongs to a God whose requirements are serious and specific. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the text is explicitly critical of the resulting religion: the syncretism is not acceptable worship of the Lord but a compromise that the narrator summarizes as "they worshiped the Lord but they also served their idols."
24.historical_context: The Assyrian policy of population transfer (deporting conquered peoples and importing subject peoples from other parts of the empire) was designed to break national identity and prevent rebellion. The peoples imported into Samaria — Babylonians, Cuthites, people from Avva, Hamath, and Sepharvaim — brought their own religious traditions. The resulting mixed population, practicing a syncretic religion, is the historical origin of the Samaritan people of the New Testament period. The deported Israelite priest who returns to Bethel represents a degraded form of Yahwism — teaching at the site of the calf-worship shrines, producing not covenant faithfulness but syncretism. The summary verse (17:41) — "they worshiped the Lord but also served their idols. To this day their children and grandchildren continue to do as their ancestors did" — explains the Samaritan religious culture that persists into Jesus's time.
24.literary_structure: The section closes chapter 17 with a catalog of imported nations and their deities, building to the summary verdict in verse 41. The structure parallels the theological explanation of verses 7–23 — there the explanation of why Israel was removed; here the explanation of what replaced Israel. The chapter thus provides a complete theological account: Israel's sin, Israel's exile, and the syncretistic compromise that fills the land in their absence. The final verse's "to this day" indicates the narrative is connecting the ancient situation to a contemporary reality the first readers would recognize.
24.theological_application: Calvin observes that the Samaritan religious settlement demonstrates that knowing what God requires is not enough — the returned priest taught the requirements, but the settlers combined them with their existing gods. This is the perennial pattern of religious syncretism: acknowledging the true God alongside other loyalties rather than in exclusive commitment. Adam Clarke notes that the fear of the Lord produced by the lions was genuine but insufficient — it was the fear of consequences, not the reverence of covenant love. For the church, this distinguishes between outward religious compliance (useful and necessary) and the complete devotion that God's covenant requires. The "fear the Lord but serve their idols" pattern is the pattern of cultural Christianity — acknowledging God in forms while the heart's actual loyalty lies elsewhere.
24.christology: The Samaritan woman's conversation with Jesus at Jacob's well (John 4) takes place in the context of this very history. She asks the question created by 2 Kings 17: "Our ancestors worshiped on this mountain, but you Jews claim that the place where we must worship is in Jerusalem." Jesus's answer — "a time is coming when you will worship the Father neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem... true worshipers will worship the Father in the Spirit and in truth" (John 4:21–23) — transcends the entire Samaritan problem. The syncretism created by Assyrian population transfer, the "fear the Lord but serve their idols" compromise, the geographic-dispute theology — all of it is superseded by worship in Spirit and truth through the Messiah who speaks to the woman at the well. The historical problem of chapter 17 is resolved by the theological gift of chapter 4 of John.
